ocr.text: In recent studies evaluating the ecological impacts of different pine species on larval survivorship, significant variations were
observed between Pinus massoniana and Pinus elliottii. The data presented in the accompanying table highlights the percentage
of larval survivorship associated with each species, along with metrics regarding previous-year and current-year needle
production. Notably, larval survivorship for P. massoniana was recorded at 46.00 ± 3.93%, showcasing a robust capacity for
supporting larval populations. In contrast, P. elliottii displayed a markedly lower survivorship rate of 19.67 ± 3.98%.
Parameters  Host species  Needle age
P. massoniana  P. elliottii  F  Previous-year needles  Current-year needles  F
These findings suggest that the ecological characteristics of each pine species can significantly influence the survivorship of
larvae, potentially due to differences in needle quality or availability. Furthermore, the table delineates the relationship between
needle age and larval survivorship, with current-year needles of P. massoniana appearing to enhance larval survival more
effectively than previous-year needles. This differentiation in survivorship rates underscores the importance of species selection
in forest management practices, particularly in relation to biodiversity conservation and the sustainability of pest populations.
Future research should further investigate the underlying mechanisms driving these differences to inform effective management
strategies.
